commit 664a99251d3d1054639f70884c159c88991acc1d
Author: Kit Dallege <xaum.io@gmail.com>

Docs/mm: document Boot Memory

This commit fills in the bootmem.rst stub as part of the structured memory
management documentation. It details the memblock allocator, region management,
iterators, and the transition to the buddy allocator.

> diff --git a/Documentation/mm/bootmem.rst b/Documentation/mm/bootmem.rst
> index eb2b31eedfa1..b20520f53603 100644
> --- a/Documentation/mm/bootmem.rst
> +++ b/Documentation/mm/bootmem.rst

[ ... ]

> +Iteration
> +---------
> +
> +Memblock provides iterator macros for walking memory ranges:
> +
> +- for_each_mem_range() iterates over free ranges (memory minus
> +  reserved).

Is this description accurate? Looking at include/linux/memblock.h,
for_each_mem_range() iterates over all memory regions, passing NULL for the
subtraction list. The macro that subtracts reserved regions to iterate over
free memory is for_each_free_mem_range().

> +- for_each_reserved_mem_region() iterates over reserved ranges.
> +- for_each_mem_pfn_range() iterates by page frame number, which is
> +  used heavily during page and zone initialization.
> +
> +These iterators handle the subtraction of reserved regions from memory
> +regions internally, presenting the caller with a simple sequence of
> +available ranges.

Do these iterators actually handle region subtraction internally? The
for_each_reserved_mem_region() macro appears to simply iterate linearly over
the memblock.reserved.regions array without performing any subtraction.

[ ... ]

> +Struct Page Initialization
> +--------------------------
> +
> +Every physical page frame needs an initialized struct page before the
> +page allocator can manage it.  On small systems this is done synchronously
> +during boot.  On large systems with hundreds of gigabytes of RAM, this
> +initialization can take a significant amount of time.
> +
> +With CONFIG_DEFERRED_STRUCT_PAGE_INIT, only pages in the boot node's
> +lower zones are initialized during early boot — enough to get the system
> +running.  The remaining pages are initialized in parallel by worker threads

Does this correctly reflect the scope of early initialization?

Looking at defer_init() in mm/mm_init.c, the kernel evaluates zones for all
available memory nodes, not just the boot node. It initializes the lower zones
of all nodes and also initializes at least one section of the highest zone for
every node before deferring the rest.

> +(via the padata framework) before they are first needed.  This can save
> +several seconds of boot time on large NUMA systems.
> +
> +Each page is initialized by setting its flags, reference count, and links
> +to the owning node and zone.  Pages in memory holes or NOMAP regions
> +are marked as reserved and are never handed to the page allocator.

commit 664a99251d3d1054639f70884c159c88991acc1d
Author: Kit Dallege <xaum.io@gmail.com>
Date:   Sat Mar 14 15:25:27 2026 +0000

    Docs/mm: document Boot Memory
    
    Fill in the bootmem.rst stub created in commit 481cc97349d6
    ("mm,doc: Add new documentation structure") as part of
    the structured memory management documentation following
    Mel Gorman's book outline.
    
    Signed-off-by: Kit Dallege <xaum.io@gmail.com>

diff --git a/Documentation/mm/bootmem.rst b/Documentation/mm/bootmem.rst
index eb2b31eedfa1..b20520f53603 100644
--- a/Documentation/mm/bootmem.rst
+++ b/Documentation/mm/bootmem.rst
@@ -3,3 +3,142 @@
 ===========
 Boot Memory
 ===========
+
+The kernel needs a memory allocator long before the page allocator is ready.
+The memblock allocator fills this role, managing physical memory from the
+earliest stages of boot until the buddy allocator takes over.  The
+implementation is in ``mm/memblock.c`` and ``mm/mm_init.c``.
+
+.. contents:: :local:
+
+Memblock
+========
+
+Memblock tracks physical memory as two arrays of regions: ``memory`` (all
+usable RAM reported by firmware) and ``reserved`` (memory already allocated
+or otherwise unavailable).  A free page is one that appears in ``memory``
+but not in ``reserved``.  These two arrays, along with global state such as
+the allocation direction and address limit, are held in a single
+``struct memblock`` instance.
+
+Each region is a ``struct memblock_region`` recording a base address, size,
+NUMA node ID, and a set of flags:
+
+- **HOTPLUG**: memory that may be physically removed at runtime.
+- **MIRROR**: memory with hardware mirroring for reliability.
+- **NOMAP**: memory that should not be directly mapped by the kernel
+  (e.g., firmware-reserved ranges that are usable but not mappable).
+- **DRIVER_MANAGED**: memory whose lifecycle is managed by a device driver.
+
+Region Management
+-----------------
+
+Firmware and architecture code populate the arrays early in boot.
+``memblock_add()`` registers a range of usable RAM.  ``memblock_reserve()``
+marks a range as taken — this is used for the kernel image itself, device
+tree blobs, initrd, and other early allocations.
+
+When regions are added, overlapping ranges are merged automatically.
+Internally, ``memblock_add_range()`` handles insertion, overlap detection,
+and merging in a single pass.  If the region array is full, it is doubled
+in size — using memblock itself to allocate the new array.
+
+``memblock_remove()`` deletes a range from the ``memory`` array (used when
+firmware reports memory that turns out to be unusable).
+``memblock_phys_free()`` removes a range from ``reserved``, making it
+available for allocation again.
+
+Allocation
+----------
+
+Memblock allocation scans the ``memory`` array for a range that does not
+overlap ``reserved``, respecting NUMA node affinity and a configurable
+address limit (``memblock.current_limit``).
+
+The search can run in two directions:
+
+- **Top-down** (default): allocates from the highest available address.
+  This keeps low memory free for devices with addressing limitations.
+- **Bottom-up**: allocates from the lowest available address.  Used on
+  some architectures during early boot to keep allocations predictable.
+
+Once a suitable range is found it is added to ``reserved``.  The main
+allocation functions are ``memblock_alloc()`` for virtual addresses and
+``memblock_phys_alloc()`` for physical addresses.  Both support NUMA-aware
+variants that prefer a specific node.
+
+Iteration
+---------
+
+Memblock provides iterator macros for walking memory ranges:
+
+- ``for_each_mem_range()`` iterates over free ranges (memory minus
+  reserved).
+- ``for_each_reserved_mem_region()`` iterates over reserved ranges.
+- ``for_each_mem_pfn_range()`` iterates by page frame number, which is
+  used heavily during page and zone initialization.
+
+These iterators handle the subtraction of reserved regions from memory
+regions internally, presenting the caller with a simple sequence of
+available ranges.
+
+Transition to the Page Allocator
+================================
+
+Once the buddy allocator is initialized, memblock releases its free pages
+via ``memblock_free_all()``.  This walks all free ranges and hands each
+page to the buddy allocator.  After this point memblock is no longer used
+for allocation and its data structures can be freed (on systems that
+support it, the memblock arrays themselves are returned to the page
+allocator via ``memblock_discard()``).
+
+Named Reservations
+------------------
+
+The ``reserve_mem`` kernel command line parameter allows firmware or boot
+loaders to reserve named memory regions that persist across kexec.  These
+are tracked separately and can be looked up by name at runtime with
+``reserve_mem_find_by_name()``.
+
+Page and Zone Initialization
+============================
+
+``mm/mm_init.c`` bridges memblock and the page allocator.  Its primary
+responsibilities are determining zone boundaries and initializing
+``struct page`` for every physical page frame.
+
+Zone Topology
+-------------
+
+The function ``free_area_init()`` is called by architecture code to set up
+nodes and zones.  It calculates zone boundaries based on architectural
+constraints (which address ranges can be used for DMA, which are always
+mapped, etc.) and kernel command line parameters:
+
+- ``kernelcore=`` sets the amount of memory that must be in non-movable
+  zones.
+- ``movablecore=`` sets the amount of memory to place in ``ZONE_MOVABLE``.
+- ``movable_node`` allows entire NUMA nodes to be treated as movable.
+- ``kernelcore=mirror`` restricts non-movable memory to mirrored regions.
+
+These parameters control the boundary between ``ZONE_MOVABLE`` and the
+other zones, which in turn affects how much memory is available for
+transparent huge pages, memory hot-remove, and CMA.
+
+Struct Page Initialization
+--------------------------
+
+Every physical page frame needs an initialized ``struct page`` before the
+page allocator can manage it.  On small systems this is done synchronously
+during boot.  On large systems with hundreds of gigabytes of RAM, this
+initialization can take a significant amount of time.
+
+With ``CONFIG_DEFERRED_STRUCT_PAGE_INIT``, only pages in the boot node's
+lower zones are initialized during early boot — enough to get the system
+running.  The remaining pages are initialized in parallel by worker threads
+(via the padata framework) before they are first needed.  This can save
+several seconds of boot time on large NUMA systems.
+
+Each page is initialized by setting its flags, reference count, and links
+to the owning node and zone.  Pages in memory holes or ``NOMAP`` regions
+are marked as reserved and are never handed to the page allocator.

Alright, the core of the matter: `mm/mm_init.c` bridges memblock and the page allocator. Its main goal is to figure out zone boundaries and initialize `struct page` for every physical page frame. The system architecture defines these boundaries, taking DMA constraints, mapping rules, and kernel parameters into account. Parameters like `kernelcore=`, `movablecore=`, `movable_node`, and `kernelcore=mirror` impact the boundary between zones, and that boundary greatly affects things like huge pages, hot-remove, and CMA.

Every physical page frame requires an initialized `struct page`, which is essential for the page allocator. Small systems do this during boot, but on larger systems, it can take ages. The clever trick here is `CONFIG_DEFERRED_STRUCT_PAGE_INIT`, which only initializes pages in the boot node's lower zones early, allowing the system to boot quickly. The remaining pages are later initialized in parallel. Very clever, saves boot time on big NUMA systems. Each page is initialized with flags, reference counts, and links to the node and zone. Memory holes or `NOMAP` regions are reserved and not given to the page allocator.

Right, the key is the boot process. During early boot, as the comment suggests, pages *are* initialized on demand, synchronously. But, after `page_alloc_init_late()` completes, the system transitions. That's when the worker threads kick in, parallelizing the initialization of the remaining pages. The commit message's phrase "before they are first needed" is accurate in that *globally*, initialization happens before the system *generally* needs the pages after boot. The multithreaded jobs are part of the `page_alloc_init_late()` function.

But, as I'm thinking through this, I see a nuance. The comment is still right; if a page is *needed* before the worker threads complete, it's initialized synchronously on-demand, just as the comment suggests. I'm starting to think this is a bit of a race condition and a carefully worded description of different initialization paths. The commit message aims to describe the *typical* scenario after the boot phase, the comment accounts for the case before the deferred initialization completes.

    "thought": "**My Thoughts on the Kernel's Early Memory Allocation**

Okay, let's refresh my memory on the memblock allocator. It's the unsung hero, the memory manager that kicks in before the buddy allocator even thinks about showing up. It's crucial because the kernel needs a way to manage physical memory from the get-go, before the more sophisticated page allocator is ready for action. The code lives in `mm/memblock.c` and `mm/mm_init.c`, as expected.

Essentially, memblock tracks physical memory using two key arrays: `memory`, representing all usable RAM identified by the firmware, and `reserved`, which holds memory that is already allocated or otherwise unavailable. A free page is simply one present in `memory` but absent from `reserved`. This crucial state, along with allocation direction and address limits, is all held within a single `struct memblock` instance.

Each memory region is meticulously described by a `struct memblock_region`. This structure neatly records the base address, size, NUMA node ID, and a set of flags. These flags are particularly interesting, they are as follows: `HOTPLUG`, indicating memory that can be removed at runtime; `MIRROR`, for memory with hardware mirroring; `NOMAP`, for memory that shouldn't be directly mapped by the kernel; and `DRIVER_MANAGED`, which signals that a device driver manages the memory's lifecycle.

The early stages of boot are all about populating these arrays. Firmware and architecture code play a crucial role here. The core functions are `memblock_add()`, which registers usable RAM ranges, and `memblock_reserve()`, which marks ranges as taken. This is used for kernel image, device tree blobs, initrd, and other essential early allocations.

An elegant touch is that overlapping regions are automatically merged during addition. Internally, `memblock_add_range()` takes care of insertion, overlap detection, and merging in a single pass. And if the region array gets full, it dynamically doubles in size, cleverly using memblock itself to allocate the new array. Neat!

Now, the opposite functions are `memblock_remove()`, used to delete ranges from the `memory` array (say, if firmware reports unusable memory) and `memblock_phys_free()` which, intriguingly, removes a range from the `reserved` array, thus making it available for allocation once again. Author: Kit Dallege <xaum.io@gmail.com>

Docs/mm: document Boot Memory

This commit fills in the bootmem.rst stub as part of the structured memory
management documentation. It details the memblock allocator, region management,
iterators, and the transition to the buddy allocator.

> diff --git a/Documentation/mm/bootmem.rst b/Documentation/mm/bootmem.rst
> index eb2b31eedfa1..b20520f53603 100644
> --- a/Documentation/mm/bootmem.rst
> +++ b/Documentation/mm/bootmem.rst

[ ... ]

> +Iteration
> +---------
> +
> +Memblock provides iterator macros for walking memory ranges:
> +
> +- for_each_mem_range() iterates over free ranges (memory minus
> +  reserved).

Is this description accurate? Looking at include/linux/memblock.h,
for_each_mem_range() iterates over all memory regions, passing NULL for the
subtraction list. The macro that subtracts reserved regions to iterate over
free memory is for_each_free_mem_range().

> +- for_each_reserved_mem_region() iterates over reserved ranges.
> +- for_each_mem_pfn_range() iterates by page frame number, which is
> +  used heavily during page and zone initialization.
> +
> +These iterators handle the subtraction of reserved regions from memory
> +regions internally, presenting the caller with a simple sequence of
> +available ranges.

Do these iterators actually handle region subtraction internally? The
for_each_reserved_mem_region() macro appears to simply iterate linearly over
the memblock.reserved.regions array without performing any subtraction.

[ ... ]

> +Struct Page Initialization
> +--------------------------
> +
> +Every physical page frame needs an initialized struct page before the
> +page allocator can manage it.  On small systems this is done synchronously
> +during boot.  On large systems with hundreds of gigabytes of RAM, this
> +initialization can take a significant amount of time.
> +
> +With CONFIG_DEFERRED_STRUCT_PAGE_INIT, only pages in the boot node's
> +lower zones are initialized during early boot — enough to get the system
> +running.  The remaining pages are initialized in parallel by worker threads

Does this correctly reflect the scope of early initialization?

Looking at defer_init() in mm/mm_init.c, the kernel evaluates zones for all
available memory nodes, not just the boot node. It initializes the lower zones
of all nodes and also initializes at least one section of the highest zone for
every node before deferring the rest.

> +(via the padata framework) before they are first needed.  This can save
> +several seconds of boot time on large NUMA systems.
